front: Work: this 584-line cyclical poem based on the Aztec calendar opens and closes with 'a willow of crystal, a poplar of water'
Work: 'I travel your body, like the world' and 'I've forgotten your name, Melusina, Laura, Isabel, Persephone, Mary'
Work: the halfway point of this poem is the line 'Madrid, 1937'
Work: 'The Sons of La Malinche' describes Mexicans as descendants of a 'violated woman' and discusses 'La Chingada'
Work: 'The Pachuco and Other Extremes' is the first essay, about Mexican-American youth subculture
Work: contains 'The Day of the Dead' and 'The Conquest and Colonialism' among its essays on Mexican identity
Work: claims solitude is 'the profoundest fact of the human condition'; discusses the macho figure as a 'mask'
Work: imagines a successful man named 'No One' and his son 'Nobody'
Work: 'Post Data' added after the Tlatelolco student massacre
Work: biography of Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz; discusses a woman who tried to 'reconcile illuminism and the Baroque'
work: The Traps of Faith (Sor Juana biography)

front: Work: named after a Heraclitus fragment about the unity of opposites; claims the poetic experience 'transmutes man into an image'
back: The Bow and the Lyre (Octavio Paz)
work: The Bow and the Lyre / Children of the Mire

front: Work: the narrator moves into his apartment with an ocean wave he meets at the beach in this prose piece from Eagle or Sun?
back: My Life with the Wave (Octavio Paz)
work: Eagle or Sun? / 'My Life with the Wave'

front: Work: analyzes the origins of language through Hanuman and a woman named 'Splendor'; inspired by a journey to Galta
Author: poetry frequently translated by his friend Eliot Weinberger
Author: collaborated with Roubaud, Sanguineti, and Tomlinson on a quadrilingual renga in a Paris basement (1969)
Author: husband of Elena Garro
Author: wrote the prologue to Alejandra Pizarnik's The Tree of Diana
Author: adapted Hawthorne's 'Rappaccini's Daughter,' inspired by Yeats and Noh theatre; a messenger matches characters with Tarot figures
Author: wrote afterword to Weinberger's study of nineteen translations of Wang Wei's 'Deer Park'
Author: characterized poetry as 'between what I see and what I say' in a letter to Roman Jakobson
Author: poem Blanco, written in Delhi, was intended to be published as a scroll of differently-colored sections
